deck_name: Pure Orcust (Tryout Duel - Time Travel 2019 | OCG TOSS Format)
deck_excerpt: They're back like they never left!
deck_description:
Play this in Master Duel's July Tryout Duel - Time Travel 2019 event! The event's banlist is based on the OCG's October 2019 banlist.

Have fun!

Chaos Monsters Dominate the Field

A compact Lightsworn package is great for fueling the graveyard. Grind out value until Black Luster Soldier and Chaos Sorcerer come down to solidify your lead.

 

Chaos Betrayer likes being milled.

Thunder Dragon puts a Light in your graveyard and gives you free discard fodder.

Chaos Witch can put herself in the Graveyard, and gets value when she's banished for the Chaos bosses.

Snipe Hunter is pretty good, and he can toss Light and Dark monsters into the graveyard.

Mystic Tomato can float into itself, Snipe Hunter, Witch of the Black Forest, or Necro Gardna/Bacon Saver.

Charge of the Light Brigade can search Garoth or Jain for beaters, Lyla for spell/trap removal, Ehren for defender removal, or Ryko for catch-all removal. Also fuels the graveyard!

Gold Sarcophagus finds your 1-of Black Luster Soldier or a Chaos Sorcerer.

The Warrior Returning Alive can retrieve Black Luster Soldier or one of your Lightsworn beaters.

My Body as a Shield is nice to have after you stick a Chaos boss on the field.

Twin Twisters and Magic Jammer have the added upside of putting Light and Dark monsters in your graveyard.

 

Points Breakdown (100/100)

Black Luster Soldier - Envoy of the Beginning x1 - 30

Chaos Sorcerer x2 - 20

Charge of the Light Brigade x2 - 14

Witch of the Black Forest x1 - 10

Chaos Betrayer x1 - 7

Chaos Witch x1 - 7

Compulsory Evacuation Device x2 - 4

Forbidden Chalice x1 - 2

Lyla, Lightsworn Sorceress x1 - 1

Necro Gardna x1 - 1

Book of Moon x1 - 1

Mystical Space Typhoon x1 - 1

Twin Twisters x1 - 1

Wiretap x1 - 1

Sidegrades: Solar Recharge, D.D. Warrior Lady, Allure of Darkness, Chaos Zone, Magical Merchant, other Lightsworn monsters

deck_excerpt: Light and Darkness Ritual deck using Branded package
deck_description:
Well, New deck time.  Yes, this build is crazy expensive, but there are clear places to cut the cost, mainly Harmonia, Sloth and Crown.  Those 8 cards would save several Hundred USD.  Otherwise its just waiting for Black Chaos to come down from its current $55-60 price point.  The main goal on T1 is to get Mind Shuffle face up with Griffoh or Black Chaos, Ragged Records and Fallen of the White Dragon both also get to Griffoh, the latter by making Crimson King to search Crimson Call, which in turn searches Griffoh.  Once you do that you search and pitch Skull Archfiend to dump Light and Darkness Ritual and add Magician of Dark Chaos - Black Chaos, use the ritual's own effect to add itself and Skull Archfiend back, then banish Griffoh or Dark Chaos to pay the ritual cost for Dark Chaos.  Mind Shuffle will add BLS - Soldier of Light and Darkness on the oponent's turn then tag in and out between them to interact with the opponent.  Celtic Mystic comes up in certain hands to serve as a Graceful Chairity and another way to summon your innitial Ritual monster.

Beyond the earlier mentioned Crimson King line, Fallen of White Dragon does a lot of its usual stuff, just now with the added option of the finally released The Three Champions Swordsouls... who do nothing for the actual swordsoul deck that makes it worth playing over Chixao, but does a lot for Branded.  I'm not really using the search effect, Cartesia and Quem are great, but don't really add anything needed here, the targeted negate and destroy is nice, though to get the destroy you need and Ecclesia or Albaz in Grave, which is just how you summon it anyway, but Ecclesia is in hand and Albaz is removed by something like a Bystial or an opposing Skull Archfiend you lose part of the effect.

 

Beyond that there isn't much to say about what is in the deck, the staples are largely the things you'd expect.  I will note I have experimented with a small package of Dark Magician of Destruction, Gaze of Timaeus and Dragoon, but cut it becuase I was legitmately hard drawing the Gaze more often than I was actually activating, the deck as presently constructed isn't really concerned about summoning Black Chaos t1, which is a necessary step to utilize this package since you should never be banishing Dark Chaos for this.  This package will be revisted in September when the Limit Over Collection cards are imported in Magnificent Monsters and we get the Dark Magician, The Pharoh's Servant and Dark Magical Curtain or whatever Konami elects to localize them as, at which point we should see a Dark Magician build of this deck become prominent and will be able to access Gaze much more consistently.

deck_excerpt: Paleo, Ashtra, Mill
deck_description:
Deck Notes

Card Change

  • Scrap-Iron Scarecrow → Corpse Seller Crow

    • https://www.masterduelmeta.com/articles/news/jun-29-2026/BETB

General Thoughts

  • Milling comes pretty easily now.

  • Going second still sucks.

    • Pot of Sloth, Sphere Mode, and Evenly Matched all try to mitigate this.

    • However, you generally lose a game if you have to start second in a bo3.

Ashtra Engine

  • Ashtra really makes you think.

    • You have to remember what each card does, and then on top what the game state is and which synchro/effects are best for you.

    • Typical line:

      • Field spell (and hopefully opening with some sendable traps and some good ones).

      • Use the two Level 7s and the Level 8 from the Field Spell.

      • Make Zombie Vampire (maybe S:P if your opps graveyard starts triggering). 

      • ALWAYS run pot of d before to not get ashed! (or bait with another ashtra card)

  • Ashtra's grind game complements Paleo wonderfully.

  • Lost Wind is in the side deck.

    • It can be bounced back by Ashtra.

    • Useful if you are struggling to summon the level 9 synchro to start your recursion.

    • With Corpse Seller Crow, though, I usually feel fine.

  • Arc Light returns Ashtra to the hand to start everything again if it gets completely removed from the game state somehow.

  • Pot of Desires rarely banishes the Ashtra Level 8s needed for Zombie Vampire.

    • Even if it does, it usually is not a huge problem.

Corpse Seller Crow

  • Lets you mill a few cards.

  • Sets something new, which is nice.

  • If it resolves, you generally end up winning by turn three.

    • (Paleo usually wins on turn three or later anyway.)

Other Card Notes

  • Mannequin Cat can almost always hit an Attribute now.

  • Can run Undying Legion.

    • However, level 7s are for synchroing which is better.

  • Fiend Griefing is useful against graveyard-focused decks like Branded.

Points of Debate

  • Am I playing too few Paleo cards?

  • Can you ever have too much milling?

    • Corpse Seller Crow felt fine.

    • It felt especially strong after turn three.

    • Maybe there is something else the deck should be doing instead.

  • What is the correct ratio of Ashtra traps?

  • When should I be worried about Ashtra monsters being removed/ how to prevent?

Please ask any questions you may have!

deck_excerpt: quasar, angelechy, synchron, junk, synchro, stardust
deck_description: Sometime ago I've posted an Angelechy Quasar deck. After some matches, I found myself bricked sometimes or losing all my play after 1 missplay due to lack of attention. Anyway, after those tests, I've decided to change some things, like the addition of Monster Reborn and Designator of Crossout to fix missplays/recover a destroyed Synchro by some effect and have one more way to add cards from deck without being stopped respectively. It was already very easy to make Quasar when your hand was good with the other version of the deck, but I added Crimson Dragon, Sifr and Crystal Clear too to have even more easy to call boss monsters. On the side deck, you still having other ways to play with Snake-Eyes Doomed and Vengeance, Nullgainer to get Witness easier if you want it, Red Supernova as other option to Crimson effect, etc. Like the last time, I'd like to remember I'm just a Quasar fan and now a new Angelechy fan too, I don't have too much experience building decks, I'm just trying to make 2 things I like be good together. Still accepting tips to make the deck better :) (Btw, english isn't my native language, so I'm sorry for any gramatical mistake and I hope I expressed myself right)

deck_name: Yugi Muto - Reflection Mirror Dark Magician Control
deck_excerpt: A Link Evolution-ready Yugi upgrade that turns Reflection Mirror into streamlined Dark Magician control.
deck_description:
This build modernizes Yugi Muto's Reflection Mirror theme for Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution while keeping Dark Magician as the centerpiece.

 

The main plan is to loop Dark Magical Circle, Eternal Soul, Magician Navigation, and Soul Servant to maintain pressure while using Spellbook cards and Magicians' Souls to keep the hand moving. The Extra Deck adds Timaeus fusion threats, Rank 7 utility, and Link finishers so the deck can convert control into game-ending pushes.

 

Built from the Yugi Muto Legacy of the Duelist page as a competitive, character-faithful upgrade for the Link Evolution card pool.

deck_name: Yugi Muto - Reflection Mirror BLS Gaia Ritual
deck_excerpt: A faster Reflection Mirror variant built around Black Luster Soldier, Gaia pressure, and ritual consistency.
deck_description:
This Link Evolution-compatible variant upgrades the Reflection Mirror/Gaia side of Yugi's card pool into a Black Luster Soldier ritual strategy.

 

Impcantations and Manju find ritual access, Beginning Knight and Evening Twilight Knight add pressure and removal, and Gateway to Chaos ties the BLS and Gaia pieces together. The Extra Deck is tuned for generic Link, Rank 8, and Gaia utility plays when the ritual line opens extra bodies.

 

The goal is a more competitive Yugi-flavored build without losing the Gaia and Black Luster Soldier identity.

deck_name: Yugi Muto - The Final Duel Magnet Block Dragon
deck_excerpt: A competitive Magnet Warrior shell using Block Dragon, Ties of the Brethren, and Rank 4 utility.
deck_description:
This deck upgrades Yugi Muto's The Final Duel list into a focused Magnet Warrior control-combo deck for Link Evolution.

 

Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta load the field and graveyard, while Ties of the Brethren creates strong early boards backed by Koa'ki Meiru Guardian and Infinite Impermanence. Block Dragon gives the deck grind power and explosive Link/Xyz follow-up, with Berserkion and Valkyrion preserving the anime boss monster identity.

 

It is built to feel like Yugi's final duel strategy, but trimmed into a cleaner competitive shell.

deck_name: Yugi Muto - Rise of Skull Servants Lightsworn Wight
deck_excerpt: A mill-heavy Wight build that pushes King of the Skull Servants into one-shot territory fast.
deck_description:
This is a competitive upgrade of Yugi's Rise of Skull Servants challenge deck, leaning into the Lightsworn/Wight graveyard engine.

 

Charge of the Light Brigade, Raiden, Uni-Zombie, Wightprince, and Wightprincess fill the graveyard quickly so King of the Skull Servants becomes a real finisher. The Zombie package adds extension through Mezuki, Doomking Balerdroch, and Necroworld Banshee, while the Extra Deck gives the deck Link, Synchro, and Rank 3 routes.

 

The result is still recognizably Skull Servants, just tuned to play at a much sharper Link Evolution power level.

deck_name: Yugi Muto - Rise of Skull Servants Zombie World Wight
deck_excerpt: Zombie World control meets Wight aggression for a stronger Link Evolution Skull Servant list.
deck_description:
This Link Evolution version turns Rise of Skull Servants into a Zombie World Wight deck with a stronger control backbone.

 

The Wight core builds huge King of the Skull Servants attacks, while Uni-Zombie, Shiranui Solitaire, Mezuki, Necroworld Banshee, and Doomking Balerdroch create recurring disruption. Zombie World plus Super Polymerization gives the deck interaction into established boards, and the Extra Deck supports both Super Poly removal and generic Zombie/Link pressure.

 

It keeps the Skull Servant identity while giving the deck more resilience and disruption.

deck_excerpt: A table-friendly Exodia race with classic final-turn drama.
deck_description: Built for Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution, this Yami Yugi deck is for casual players who still love the suspense of drawing one more card and hoping the last Exodia piece appears. It keeps the classic assemble-the-forbidden-one fantasy front and center, with enough draw power and stall tools to make each duel feel like a little anime countdown.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Wasteland (Triamid Rock control)
deck_excerpt: Field-spell rock swapping with old-school Wasteland flavor.
deck_description: This fun/casual Yami Yugi remix turns The Wasteland into a Triamid rock-control deck where the field spells keep changing the pace of the duel. It is built for Link Evolution players who enjoy steady setup, sturdy monsters, and the satisfying moment when Triamid Sphinx finally stomps onto the table.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Attack the Moon (Onomat Rock Block Dragon)
deck_excerpt: Onomat rocks and Block Dragon turns for moon-shot board swings.
deck_description: Attack the Moon becomes a playful Onomat Rock deck for casual players who like their turns to build from oddball starters into big payoff monsters. Built for Link Evolution, it keeps the anime-name charm while giving the deck real Rank plays, rock value, and the occasional Block Dragon comeback moment.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Yugi's Friends (Silent Gadget control)
deck_excerpt: Gadgets and Silent cards make a friendly Yugi toolbox.
deck_description: Yugi's Friends is a casual toolbox deck where Gadgets, Silent cards, and familiar Yugi-style support all feel like they brought something useful to the duel. Built for Link Evolution, it is meant for players who enjoy steady advantage, simple decisions, and a warm character-deck vibe over one-track combo racing.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Fire the Catapult (Rock Stun Catapult)
deck_excerpt: Sturdy rock stun that fires off big Catapult moments.
deck_description: Fire the Catapult is a fun/casual rock-stun deck for players who like slowing the duel down, sticking tough monsters, and turning the corner with nostalgic pressure. It is Link Evolution compatible and pitched for table games where the best part is making an old-school theme feel surprisingly sturdy.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Kuriboh Power (Kuriboh Link control)
deck_excerpt: Kuriboh chaos, Link climbing, and lots of small-card mischief.
deck_description: Kuriboh Power is here for players who love the silly side of Yugi decks: tiny monsters, surprise protection, and a game plan that turns cute cards into real Link pressure. It is a casual Link Evolution list made to create memorable turns, protect your life points, and let the little fuzzballs do more than anyone expects.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Black Luster Soldier (Chaos Thunder BLS)
deck_excerpt: Thunder Dragons charge up a splashy Black Luster Soldier finish.
deck_description: This fun/casual BLS take uses Thunder Dragon energy to make the deck feel explosive without turning it into another straight ritual shell. It is built for Link Evolution players who want banish-zone sparks, Chaos monsters, and a dramatic Black Luster Soldier payoff that still feels like a Yami Yugi boss moment.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Skull Dragon (Red-Eyes Skull fusion)
deck_excerpt: Black Skull Dragon nostalgia with Red-Eyes Fusion flair.
deck_description: The Skull Dragon leans into one of the best casual Yu-Gi-Oh! feelings: two iconic monsters joining forces for a flashy Fusion summon. This Link Evolution deck gives Red-Eyes and Summoned Skull fans a flavorful, table-ready plan with enough modern support to make the old favorites feel exciting again.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Magician of Chaos LOD (Chaos MAX ritual)
deck_excerpt: Magician of Black Chaos MAX brings big ritual drama.
deck_description: This Legacy of the Duelist ritual build is for casual duelists who want Magician of Black Chaos MAX to feel ceremonial, powerful, and worth building around. The deck keeps the Dark Magician atmosphere intact while giving players a clearer path to those big ritual-summon moments.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Magician of Chaos LE (Dark Magician ritual control)
deck_excerpt: Dark Magician and Chaos rituals share the same stage.
deck_description: This Link Evolution version blends Dark Magician control with Magician of Chaos ritual plays for players who want the theme to feel theatrical and interactive. It is built for casual games where summoning the right spellcaster at the right moment matters more than racing through the same line every duel.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - A Chain Destruction (Chain Burn)
deck_excerpt: Set the chain, light the fuse, and count the damage.
deck_description: A Chain Destruction becomes a casual Chain Burn deck for players who enjoy timing, bluffing, and the little thrill of watching damage add up one link at a time. Built for Link Evolution, it is best for tables that enjoy puzzle-like trap sequencing and old-school burn drama.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Dark Magician Girl LOD (Magician Girl control)
deck_excerpt: Magician Girls protect each other in a cozy control shell.
deck_description: This Dark Magician Girl list is a casual spellcaster showcase built for players who want the Magician Girls to actually matter in the duel. The Legacy of the Duelist card pool gives it control tools, support spells, and enough Yugi flavor to make every summon feel like part of the show.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Dark Magician Girl LE (DMG fusion combo)
deck_excerpt: Dark Magician Girl gets a Fusion-combo spotlight.
deck_description: This Link Evolution Dark Magician Girl deck keeps the mood light, nostalgic, and splashy, with Dragon Knight and Quintet-style Fusion lines giving the deck its big moments. It is for casual players who want a characterful spellcaster deck that can still build toward exciting finishes.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Deck Destruction (Danger Dark World discard)
deck_excerpt: Danger and Dark World make discard turns wild and funny.
deck_description: Deck Destruction is the messy, memorable kind of casual deck: hands change, monsters appear, and every discard can turn into a story. This Link Evolution version uses Danger and Dark World momentum to make the theme active, unpredictable, and fun for players who enjoy controlled chaos.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Dark Magicians (Spellbook Prophecy control)
deck_excerpt: Spellbook pages replace traps with wizard-school control.
deck_description: Dark Magicians is reworked as a Spellbook Prophecy deck for casual players who like spellcaster control with a little library-magic flair. It is Link Evolution compatible, built around searching, sequencing, and protecting your setup, while still feeling like a Yami Yugi spellcaster duel full of clever answers.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Sky Dragon (Slifer True Name control)
deck_excerpt: Stack the deck, call The True Name, and summon Slifer.
deck_description: The Sky Dragon is all about the casual thrill of landing Slifer and making the table notice. This Link Evolution deck uses Card Advance, The True Name, and control tools to help the Egyptian God feel like a real centerpiece rather than just a card you hope to draw someday.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Fiend's Sanctuary (Lair BA fiends)
deck_excerpt: Lair of Darkness gives Yami a fiendish BA playground.
deck_description: The Fiend's Sanctuary becomes a casual Lair of Darkness and Burning Abyss fiend deck, full of tribute tricks, graveyard value, and shadowy board control. Built for Link Evolution, it is for players who want the Yami side of Yugi to feel sneaky, resilient, and a little theatrical.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Ceremonial Duel (God Monarch tribute)
deck_excerpt: Monarch tributes turn the ceremonial duel into god-card theater.
deck_description: The Ceremonial Duel is designed for players who want the endgame to feel like an event: tribute, summon, and let an Egyptian God define the duel. This Link Evolution version uses a Monarch-style tribute engine to make the divine monsters feel more reachable in casual games.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Legendary Duelist (Chaos Lightsworn BLS)
deck_excerpt: Lightsworn milling fuels a chaotic Black Luster Soldier finale.
deck_description: The Legendary Duelist keeps Black Luster Soldier at the center while using Chaos Lightsworn energy to fill the Graveyard and create splashy comeback turns. It is a casual Link Evolution deck for players who enjoy risk, milling, and the feeling that the next card could unlock something huge.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Heart of the Dragons LOD (Timaeus Dark Magician)
deck_excerpt: Timaeus gives Dark Magician a classic dragon-fusion finish.
deck_description: Heart of the Dragons is a casual Dark Magician deck with The Eye of Timaeus fantasy dialed up: spellcaster setup, dramatic Fusion moments, and familiar Yami Yugi flavor. Built for Legacy of the Duelist, it aims to feel cool to pilot even when the duel becomes a back-and-forth story.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Heart of the Dragons LE (Critias Lair trap fusion)
deck_excerpt: Critias and Lair turn trap cards into surprise dragons.
deck_description: This Link Evolution Heart of the Dragons build gives casual players the joy of flipping traps and turning them into flashy Critias Fusion monsters. Lair of Darkness adds a shadowy control angle, making the deck feel tricky, flavorful, and full of surprise reveals.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Legendary Timaeus (Legendary Knight Hermos)
deck_excerpt: Legendary Knights and Hermos take the anime stage.
deck_description: The Legendary Timaeus is built for casual players who want the Legendary Knights and Hermos cards to feel like real story pieces. This Link Evolution deck is not shy about spectacle: equip, fuse, reveal, and enjoy the big character-deck moments that make these cards memorable.

deck_excerpt: Aroma life-gain control for relaxed garden-table duels.
deck_description: Aromage gives the Yami Yugi lineup a softer casual option: gain life, grow value, and let the plant engine slowly take over. It is Link Evolution compatible and perfect for players who enjoy steady resource games, soothing theme flavor, and wins that bloom one small trigger at a time.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - Fang of Critias (Traptrix Artifact Critias)
deck_excerpt: Traptrix and Artifacts set the stage for Critias surprises.
deck_description: Fang of Critias is for casual players who love when a trap card suddenly becomes a dragon. This Link Evolution deck blends Traptrix setup, Artifact disruption, and Critias spectacle into a fun trap-heavy shell with plenty of surprise moments.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Chaos (Gren Maju Chaos banish)
deck_excerpt: Banish big, swing bigger with casual Chaos fireworks.
deck_description: The Chaos is a fun/casual banish deck built around the simple joy of making one attack matter a lot. Gren Maju and Chaos tools give the list explosive comeback potential in Link Evolution, making it a great pick when you want wild numbers, risky lines, and big table reactions.

deck_name: Yami Yugi - The Spellcasters' Stone (Endymion spell counter)
deck_excerpt: Spell counters pile up for Endymion wizard-board fun.
deck_description: The Spellcasters' Stone turns Yami Yugi's magic theme into an Endymion spell-counter playground. Built for Link Evolution, it rewards casual players who enjoy stacking counters, managing resources, and watching a board of spellcasters grow from a few careful activations into a full magical engine.

Lair of Darkness, Lilith, Ahrima, and Infernoids keep the table tense while Sphere Mode and The Winged Dragon of Ra create the big villain-boss moments. It is a public Non-Meta Deck for spicy character duels, dramatic tributes, and memorable finishes rather than current tournament play.

deck_description: Built for Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution, this Yami Marik deck is a fun/casual fiend pile with a nasty personality. Unchained monsters blow up your own cards for value, Burning Abyss keeps resources moving, and Lair of Darkness plus Darkest Diabolos gives the deck that shadowy Marik finish. Tour Guide starts the climb, Graff, Cir, and Scarm keep resources moving, Dante and Beatrice make the Graveyard feel alive, and the Phantom Knights package adds Fog Blade interaction plus Break Sword swings. deck_name: Yami Bakura - FINAL: Destiny Board Dark Sanctuary
deck_excerpt: Protect the board, spell FINAL, and make the whole table watch the clock.
deck_description: Built for Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution, this public Non-Meta Deck keeps Bakura's FINAL win condition front and center. Dark Sanctuary, Destiny Board, Sentence of Doom, Cardcar D, and protective traps make it a dedicated casual stall-control deck rather than a generic good-stuff pile. It is for players who enjoy alternate win conditions, tense countdown turns, and the wonderful panic of seeing another Spirit Message hit the field.

deck_name: Weevil Underwood - Moth Evolution: Giant Ballpark Moth
deck_excerpt: Swarm tiny insects, wake the Moth, and let Giant Ballpark turn every battle into trouble.
deck_description: Built for Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution, this public Non-Meta Deck turns Weevil's original Moth Evolution idea into a faster Giant Ballpark shell. Gokipole, Resonance Insect, Cocoon of Ultra Evolution, Metamorphosed Insect Queen, and Perfectly Ultimate Great Moth keep the classic bug-boss fantasy alive while the modern Insect engine gives the deck real pressure. It is for casual duelists who want Weevil flavor, surprise swarms, and a big old Moth that actually has a way to hit the table.

Sting the Poison, Pin the Bullseye, Twinbow, Hama, and Azusa create quick Insect pressure, while Cocoon of Ultra Evolution and the Moth package keep the old-school Weevil identity visible. deck_name: Weevil Underwood - Insect Queen: Traptrix Queen Control
deck_excerpt: Trap Holes, DNA Surgery, and Insect Queen make every attack feel like a mistake.
deck_description: Built for Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution, this public Non-Meta Deck modernizes Weevil's Insect Queen strategy as a Traptrix and Giant Ballpark control deck. Myrmeleo, Mantis, Sera, Trap Hole cards, DNA Surgery, Insect Barrier, and Metamorphosed Insect Queen all point toward a table where the opponent's monsters keep wandering into bad places. It is for casual control players who enjoy traps, bug royalty, and making combat feel wonderfully unsafe.
